title: Spinning alpaca top into fingering-weight 2-ply
subtitle: Controlling a low-elasticity, high-lustre fibre to produce a smooth plied yarn
excerpt: Huacaya alpaca top has long, soft staples (8-15 cm) and almost no crimp, which means it behaves very differently from wool at the wheel. A snug pinch, a slow and deliberate draw, and a slightly warmer setting soak all compensate for the lack of elasticity. Two singles at around 32 WPI ply to approximately 16 WPI, a fingering weight with the drape and sheen alpaca is known for.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The lower-ply-twist rule for alpaca and the warmer-soak setting step are the standard adjustments taught across modern fibre-arts guilds. The handling differences between alpaca and wool at the wheel are documented in commercial top suppliers' notes and spinning guild resources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The more common of the two alpaca breeds, producing a dense, fluffy fleece with a crimped-looking appearance at the lock level but very little fibre-level crimp under a microscope. Huacaya fibre is soft and lustrous with low elasticity. The rarer Suri breed produces longer, straighter locks with a silkier drape. Most commercially available alpaca top is huacaya.

Fibre with little or no natural wave along the individual strand. Alpaca, silk, and many longwool breeds are low-crimp. Low-crimp fibres produce smooth, dense, lustrous yarn but have less elasticity than high-crimp wools such as Merino. This affects spinning (the fibres slide rather than lock together), plying (less ply twist is needed to balance the yarn), and finishing (the finished cloth drapes rather than bouncing back).

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Tie hair back, roll sleeves, no necklaces near the drive band, flyer, or bobbin. Loose fibres caught in spinning gear stop the wheel.

content.text: and that single fact changes how it behaves at the wheel compared to wool. Without crimp, the fibres have no natural tendency to grip each other as they draft; they slide freely, which makes it easy to draft too fast and produce a singles that is unevenly thin. The same low-crimp property means that alpaca yarn needs less ply twist than wool to achieve a balanced 2-ply: the fibres compress slightly under ply twist, and over-plying produces a harsh, stiff yarn.

top: soft and dense with a slight fluffiness at the surface. Two singles at around 32 WPI ply to approximately 16 WPI, a fingering weight. The finished yarn has the drape and sheen alpaca is known for and suits lightweight garments where that drape matters, particularly shawls and cowls.

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony)
items.qty: 1, set to a twist ratio of 10:1

content.text: Alpaca top for fine singles needs splitting into narrow strips: no more than 5 mm wide for a consistent fingering-weight draw. Because the fibres slide easily, a wider strip will draft unevenly in places, producing a singles with a noticeable slub pattern. Divide the top and set the strips aside in order so you work through them consistently.

Set the wheel on a high-ratio whorl (10:1 or higher) with light brake tension. Alpaca singles is weaker than wool singles at the same diameter: no crimp means the fibres do not interlock. The yarn should advance onto the bobbin with almost no pull. Firm take-up will break alpaca at thin spots far more than it would break wool.

The key adjustment for alpaca is the pinch. With wool, the pinch hand can be fairly relaxed because the fibre's crimp creates resistance. With alpaca, a snug pinch is necessary to prevent the fibres from slipping through the twist zone too quickly. The front hand closes firmly and stays closed for slightly longer than it would when spinning wool. Draw back slowly: about 6-8 cm per treadling cycle, rather than the 10-12 cm that suits a medium-crimp wool.

Spin Z-twist. Aim for 10-11 twists per inch on the singles: enough to hold the fibres together, but not so much that the singles feels wiry. Alpaca has high tensile strength despite low crimp, so it will not break at that twist count, but over-twisting makes the fibre difficult to ply back to a balanced yarn.

A slub forms when the pinch hand opens too soon. A clump of untwisted fibre enters the twist zone at once. If you spot one forming, stop treadling. Draw the section back slightly, let the twist take it, then carry on. Keep slubs off the bobbin. They weaken the singles and it may snap during plying.

Set both bobbins on the lazy kate and ply S-twist at a ratio of approximately 6:1. Alpaca needs less ply twist than wool because the fibres compress rather than spring back under ply pressure. A useful check: let a 30 cm loop hang free after every few metres of plying. A balanced alpaca 2-ply hangs straight or with only the slightest curl. If it curls strongly, the ply ratio is too high; slow the treadle or advance the yarn more quickly to reduce twists per inch at the ply stage.

Wind the plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy and tie in three places. Soak in water at around 40 degrees C (slightly warmer than the lukewarm water used for wool). Alpaca's scales are smoother than wool's and respond better to slightly warmer water for twist setting. Soak for 20 minutes. Remove, press gently in a towel without wringing (alpaca is more prone to matting than wool when agitated wet), and hang under no tension to dry. Do not snap the hank as you would a wool hank: the low-crimp fibres do not benefit from it and the snap can distort the yarn structure.

Measure WPI after drying. A fingering-weight alpaca 2-ply sits at approximately 16 WPI. Because alpaca has no crimp, the dry yarn may feel noticeably heavier and drapier than a wool yarn at the same WPI. This is characteristic, not a spinning error.

items.problem: The singles breaks frequently during spinning
items.cause: Take-up tension is too high, or the strip is too narrow in sections, creating weak spots below the minimum diameter for the twist count.
items.fix: Reduce the brake tension so the yarn advances onto the bobbin with almost no pull. If breaks happen at the same section of fibre each time, check for a thin spot or a loose clump in the top: draft that section out separately by hand before joining it to the main supply.

items.problem: The finished yarn is stiff and does not drape
items.cause: Singles or ply over-twisted. Alpaca compresses under excess twist and loses its characteristic soft drape.
items.fix: Reduce both the singles twist count (to 9-10 tpi) and the ply ratio on the next batch. If the current yarn is already finished, a longer soak at 40 degrees C with gentle squeezing (not wringing) sometimes softens the hand slightly.

items.problem: The hank tangles badly during the soak
items.cause: Hank ties were too loose or too few, allowing the hank to spread and the yarns to cross.
items.fix: Tie alpaca hanks in four places rather than three, and use a figure-of-eight tie rather than a simple loop. The figure-of-eight holds the bundle of yarns in two groups, preventing them from sliding past each other in the water.
